chunk_summary: This section outlines post-discharge management of electrolyte derangements in preterm, LBW, and ELBW infants, emphasizing micronutrient support, lab monitoring, and clinical pathways for addressing hypochloremia and other imbalances.
learning_objectives: Understand the importance of monitoring electrolytes in preterm/LBW/ELBW infants post-discharge.
Identify clinical pathways for managing hypochloremia and other electrolyte imbalances.
Learn recommended micronutrient supplementation strategies for preterm infants after NICU discharge.
key_facts: Hypochloremia in preterm infants can lead to growth failure and metabolic alkalosis (PMC7281990).
Micronutrient supplementation post-discharge includes iron, vitamin D, and calcium to support growth and development (AAP guidelines).
Electrolyte monitoring should be performed regularly, especially in infants with evolving BPD (Brigham and Women’s Hospital guideline).
Low birth weight infants require tailored feeding strategies to meet their nutritional needs (Government of India guideline).
Preterm infants are at higher risk for electrolyte imbalances due to immature renal function and increased losses.
reference_quote: Hypochloremia in preterm infants: clinical consequences and management — 'Hypochloremia can result in metabolic alkalosis and growth failure, requiring careful monitoring and intervention.' (PMC7281990)
document_key_topics: Lab monitoring protocols for LBW/ELBW infants
